title: Green oak three-legged stool
subtitle: A riven-oak stool with drawknife-shaped legs and a moisture-differential mortise joint
excerpt: A three-legged stool in green oak uses the moisture differential between the dried tenon and the green mortise to create a joint that locks permanently as the seat dries. The seat is a riven slab; the legs are rived, drawknife-shaped on the shaving horse, and assembled with taper tenons driven into the wet seat. This advanced project combines riving, side-axe hewing, drawknife and spokeshave shaping, and the moisture-gradient joinery at the heart of the green-woodwork chair tradition.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for riven-oak furniture construction. Jenkin, The Agricultural and Forest Industries of Wales (1901) for the Welsh stick-chair mortise-and-tenon tradition.

content.text: The three-legged stool is the foundational piece of the green-woodwork furniture tradition: three
riven
legs, a riven seat slab, and joints that tighten themselves. The seat is bored while still
green

and the leg tenons are dried before driving in. As the wet seat closes on the dried tenon over the following weeks, the joint locks permanently without glue or wedge. Three legs also find their own equilibrium on uneven ground; a four-legged stool wobbles where a three-legged one does not.
What you need: wood

For the seat: a green oak slab riven from a short log, 35 to 40 cm across and 4 to 5 cm thick, from the widest, straightest-grained section of the log. For the legs: three green oak poles, each 40 to 42 cm long, 30 to 35 mm at the tenon end. Ash gives a slightly lighter and more flexible leg; elm is more difficult to rive but very durable. Oak is the traditional choice for durability in barn and outdoor use.
What you need: tools
A froe and beetle to rive seat and leg blanks. A side axe on a side-axe block to flatten the seat top. A
drawknife
for the main leg shaping. A spokeshave for the final taper. A
shaving horse
throughout. A brace and bit for the mortise holes. Boiled linseed oil to finish.
Safety

Keep feet behind the froe and beetle strike zone when riving. On the shaving horse, keep the foot pedal firmly depressed throughout each drawknife pass; releasing it while the blade is in contact lets the work shift.
The work
Step 1: Rive the seat slab and leg blanks

Halve a short log of 40 to 50 cm diameter with the froe, then rive the halves to produce a seat slab of the target dimensions. Rive the three leg blanks separately from the same log or from a matched straight-grained round. Each leg blank should be a rough triangular section, about 45 mm across, with grain running end to end.
Step 2: Flatten the seat top

Lay the seat slab on the side-axe block with the top face up. Score relief cuts across it every 10 cm with the corner of the axe head, then hew between them to bring the face to a consistent flat plane. The side axe leaves a faceted surface; this is the working finish for a barn stool. Smooth the face with a drawknife pass if a cleaner surface is wanted.
Step 3: Drawknife and spokeshave the legs

Clamp each leg blank in the shaving horse. Drawknife the corners off the triangular section to produce a rough octagon, working from foot toward tenon end with the grain. Bring the octagon to a rough round, then switch to the spokeshave for the final taper: 30 mm diameter at the tenon end, 22 mm at the foot, over 40 cm. Work in full-length overlapping passes, rotating the blank between passes. Hone the drawknife on the India oilstone after every fifteen passes; green oak dulls edges quickly.
Step 4: Dry the tenon ends

Stand the legs upright in a warm room with the tenon ends uppermost for a week. The top 10 cm of each leg dries faster than the body. The dried tenon, being already close to its final small diameter, will absorb moisture from the green seat when driven in and expand to lock the joint. Do not dry the full leg uniformly or the joint effect is lost.
Step 5: Bore the mortises and assemble

Mark three mortise positions on the underside of the green seat slab, spaced evenly at 120-degree intervals, set in 6 cm from the rim. Bore each mortise with a brace and bit at an outward splay of 12 to 15 degrees from vertical; a bevel gauge set to this angle guides the brace direction. Reduce the tenon end of each leg to 22 mm diameter and 45 mm length with the sloyd knife. Drive each tenon into its mortise with the beetle until the shoulder seats firmly. Set the stool on a flat surface; mark any high leg with a compass, and trim it down with the drawknife.
Finishing

Apply two coats of boiled linseed oil to the seat top and all leg surfaces. Allow thirty minutes between coats and wipe off the second coat before it becomes tacky. The oil protects the oak while the mortise joints complete their drying cycle.
Variations
A saddled seat, hollowed with a travisher before the mortises are bored, is more comfortable for long use and does not require the slab to be thicker than 5 cm.
Four legs give more stability on flat floors; bore the outer two pairs at matched angles and level all four feet with the compass-and-trim method.
Care

Re-oil once a year. Any initial movement in the legs will stop as the seat dries over its first few months; do not force a slightly loose leg until the first full drying season has passed, as the joint may still be tightening.

items.fix: Remove the leg. Dry the tenon end in a warm oven at 95 degrees C for 20 minutes. Coat it with a little boiled linseed oil. Drive it back into the mortise while the seat is still at room temperature.
items.cause: The tenon was not dried before assembly, so it shrank rather than expanding into the mortise.
items.symptom: A leg works loose after assembly

items.fix: Bore mortises at least 4 cm in from the rim of the slab. Use a bit no larger than two-thirds of the slab thickness in diameter.
items.cause: The mortise bore hit a concealed shake or the slab was too thin for the bore diameter.
items.symptom: The seat slab splits during assembly
